title: Spinning bamboo viscose top
subtitle: Handling a long, slippery staple that collapses into pencil-thin drafts under its own weight
excerpt: Bamboo viscose top has a staple length of 12-15 cm and a smooth, almost frictionless surface. On the wheel, narrow drafts collapse under their own weight before the twist reaches them. A long draw at low tension is the most reliable approach; blending 30% bamboo into a merino base is easier for newcomers to the fibre. The finished yarn is lustrous and drapes well for weaving and lightweight garments.
sourceNotes: Bamboo viscose top behaviour at the wheel (long slippery staple, collapse tendency, drape of the finished yarn) is documented across commercial fibre suppliers' guidance and spinning guild workshop notes. The 30% blend recommendation is widely used in introductory bamboo spinning instruction.

glossaryTerms.definition: The length of individual fibres in a prepared top or lock, measured in centimetres. Bamboo viscose has an unusually long staple (12-15 cm) compared to most wool tops (4-12 cm). Long-staple fibres can be slippery to draft because the fibres slide past each other with little friction.

The process of drawing fibres apart lengthways to thin them before twist enters the strand. The drafting zone is the section of fibre between the spinner's two hands. Very slippery long-staple fibres like bamboo can draft too quickly if the pinch is not firm.

The reflective sheen on a fibre or yarn surface. Bamboo viscose has high lustre because the smooth, round cross-section of each fibre reflects light uniformly. Lustre is distinct from sheen in wool: it indicates a very smooth fibre surface rather than simply a fibre with some shine.

attrs.title: Wheel safety
attrs.body: Keep loose hair and clothing back from the drive band and flyer. The flyer turns fast; tie hair back and roll sleeves.

content.text: Bamboo viscose top has two properties that make it challenging to spin. First, the
staple length
of 12-15 cm is long enough that the
drafting

zone can extend a long way before the fibres run out. Second, the surface is so smooth that individual fibres barely grip each other. These two properties together mean that narrow drafts drawn at speed will simply continue extending until the fibres separate completely, because there is nothing to stop them. The strand collapses before the twist can consolidate it.
The finished yarn has beautiful
lustre
and drapes extremely well. It is well suited to weaving projects where that drape matters: lightweight shawls, scarves, and summer fabric. It is less suited to knitting where elasticity is needed.
What you need

items.name: Spinning wheel (castle or Saxony), set to a low ratio (6:1 to 7:1)
Soft tape measure for checking staple length and WPI

content.text: Option A: the merino blend (recommended for first time)

Split the bamboo top lengthways into strips 5-6 mm wide. Do the same with the merino. Alternate one strip of bamboo with two strips of merino, holding them together loosely at the join end. Feed them together through your fingers so the merino fibres grip the bamboo fibres and provide some internal friction. The merino carries the blend through the drafting zone; the bamboo adds its lustre and drape to the finished yarn.
Set the wheel to a low to moderate take-up. The blend drafts more like wool than pure bamboo, but still benefits from a slower draw and slightly less treadle speed than you would use for pure merino.
Option B: pure bamboo on a long draw
Step 1: Split the bamboo top into wider strips than you would use for wool: 10-12 mm wide. The wider strip gives more fibre mass in the drafting zone, which resists the collapse tendency.

Step 2: Set the wheel to a very low take-up and a low ratio (6:1). You need the twist to arrive quickly but not aggressively. A high take-up will snap the draft before it has time to build twist through the strand.

Step 3: Use a supported long draw. Pinch the strip firmly at the orifice-side hand and draw back slowly and steadily with the other hand. Keep the draw movement slow: 3-4 seconds per 15 cm of extension. Let the twist run into the drafting zone as you draw. Because the bamboo fibres are so long, the twist can travel a long way back into the supply before the strand begins to thin.

Step 4: Once the draft is drawn and the twist has consolidated, release the yarn onto the bobbin. Do not rush this step: the twist in bamboo takes a moment longer to settle than in wool because the smooth fibres do not grip each other as quickly.
Finishing

Wind off onto a niddy-noddy and soak in cool to lukewarm water for 20 minutes. Bamboo does not need hot water to set the twist. Press in a towel without wringing: the smooth fibres can shift against each other during agitation. Hang under light tension (a small weight at the base of the skein) to encourage the natural drape and reduce any spiral tendency from the spin twist. Measure WPI after drying: pure bamboo DK singles typically sits at 14-16 WPI.
Common problems

items.problem: The draft collapses before the twist reaches it
items.cause: The strip is too narrow, or the draw speed is too fast relative to the treadle rate.
items.fix: Widen the strip to 10-12 mm and slow the draw to a deliberate 3-4 seconds per 15 cm. If the problem persists, increase the wheel ratio slightly so twist arrives faster.

items.problem: The finished yarn has very low twist and feels limp
items.cause: The slow draw needed to avoid collapse has produced fewer twists per inch than the yarn needs for stability.
items.fix: Increase treadle speed while keeping the draw rate constant. Check the TPI: a DK-weight bamboo singles needs at least 4-5 twists per inch to hold together in use.

items.problem: The yarn is uneven and slubby throughout
items.cause: The very slippery fibres are drafting unevenly: some sections draft too quickly, others resist.
items.fix: Pre-draft the strip by hand before sitting at the wheel: gently stretch it lengthways until it is an even, attenuated roving. This pre-drafting step smooths out the thick-and-thin variation before spinning begins.
